channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In NATO’s Next Act, Can Europe Play the Leading Role?
item.description: The focus at a leaders’ summit in Turkey this week will be on building a new model for the alliance, as President Trump pushes to do less and pressures others do more.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Lara Jakes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: NATO leaders at last year’s summit in The Hague, where member states committed to increase spending on defense sharply by 2035.

item.title: Joining a Human Tide of Mourners Flowing Through Tehran’s Streets
item.description: After days of public mourning ceremonies in the Iranian capital, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s coffin arrived in Tehran’s Azadi Square. This is what I saw covering the miles-long procession on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Emile Ducke
item.media:description: Crowds gathered around the coffin of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Tehran, Iran, on Monday.

item.title: A Long-Awaited Verdict for Marine Le Pen Could Reshape France’s Politics
item.description: A court will decide on Tuesday whether to uphold Ms. Le Pen’s embezzlement conviction. If it does, the far-right leader will probably be barred from running for the presidency next year.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen met with supporters at a country fair in Liévin, France, on Saturday.

item.title: At Least 15 Dead in China After Severe Flooding During Typhoon Maysak
item.description: Videos showed inundated roads, farmland and buildings, and floodwaters rushing toward homes, while the region braced for a potential super typhoon this week.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:description: A flooded riverside near a wharf on the Yongjiang river in Nanning, in China’s southern Guangxi Province, on Monday.

item.title: Turkey Hosts NATO Summit While Cracking Down on Critics
item.description: In the weeks leading up to the two-day event, the Turkish authorities blocked websites, jailed scores of people over allegations of terrorism and arrested a prominent comedian.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard and Safak Timur
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Protesters demanding the release of Deniz Goktas, a comedian who was arrested after making jokes about President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey.

item.title: Russia Strikes Kyiv, Ukraine’s Capital, on Eve of NATO Summit
item.description: Ballistic missiles were fired into the capital of Ukraine as Russia launched its second major attack in less than a week. At least 16 people were killed, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall, Stanislav Kozliuk and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: A building damaged by a Russian missile and drone attack in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Monday.

item.title: Trump’s Intervention in Balogun’s World Cup Suspension Adds to Conflict With Europe
item.description: The decision to reverse a one-game ban landed badly in Europe, at a moment of already-heightened geopolitical tension with the United States.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Ali Watkins
item.media:description: The U.S. forward Folarin Balogun, right, whose red card in a World Cup match last week brought a one-game suspension, was cleared to play by soccer’s governing body.

item.title: With $8 Billion in Venezuelan Oil Money, U.S. Gives $300 Million in Quake Aid
item.description: In 2010, there was a huge U.S. aid effort after a quake in Haiti. Today, U.S. officials say they oversee billions in Venezuelan oil revenue, but aid is less.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers and family members searched for survivors in a building in La Guaira, Venezuela, that collapsed after back-to-back earthquakes struck the country in June.

item.title: Will Prince Harry’s Trip to Britain Repair or Deepen the Royal Rift?
item.description: Harry arrived in London Monday night for a visit that has already been overshadowed by drama and confusion over his plans.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Prince Harry and his wife, Meghan, visiting Sydney, Australia, in April. It is unclear whether Meghan and their two children will join Harry on his trip to Britain this week.

item.title: China Test Fires Long-Range Ballistic Missile in the Pacific
item.description: China’s firing of a submarine-launched missile came as Australia secured more defense deals with Pacific Island nations. Countries expressed concern about the test.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim and Chris Buckley
item.media:credit: Chinese People’s Liberation Army News and Communication Center

item.title: Former Syrian Officials Found Guilty in Torture of Pro-Democracy Protesters
item.description: The case against the two men, who have been living in Austria since 2015, was one of the first that the country has brought under the principle of universal jurisdiction.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Hussam Hammoud
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Marylise Vigneau for The New York Times
item.media:description: Khaled al-Halabi, a former intelligence commander under the dictator Bashar al-Assad, after a hearing in Vienna last month. Mr. al-Halabi was convicted of torture among other charges.

item.title: Erdogan-Trump Friendship Can Help NATO, Turkey’s Foreign Minister Says
item.description: In an interview, Turkey’s top diplomat, Hakan Fidan, said the relationship between President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and President Trump could ease NATO tensions.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.media:description: President Trump with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey in the White House, last year. While directly criticizing some NATO leaders, Mr. Trump has maintained cordial ties with Mr. Erdogan.

item.title: Riot in Sri Lanka’s Negombo prison leaves at least 23 dead
item.description: The country has faced criticism from human rights activists for the conditions in its prisons, including overcrowding and failing to protect inmates.
item.dc:creator: Pamodi Waravita and Jeremy W. Peters
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Ishara S. Kodikara/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Personnel from Sri Lanka’s Special Task Force outside the Negombo prison near Colombo on Monday, following clashes between inmates and guards.

item.title: Fatigue, Tension, Joy: England Pulls a World Cup All-Nighter
item.description: The game against Mexico started at 2 a.m. in Britain. Pubs were allowed to stay open unusually late for fans to share the drama together.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Mary Turner
item.media:credit: Mary Turner for The New York Times

item.title: New Strikes on Ships in Strait of Hormuz Test U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire
item.description: A U.S. official said Iranian missiles struck two commercial ships. There was no immediate comment from Iranian officials on the reported attacks.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Qasim Nauman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels anchored near Muscat, Oman, last month.

item.title: Trump Wanted a U.S. Soccer Star to Play in the World Cup. FIFA Found a Way.
item.description: There was nothing unusual about a call from President Trump or the reversal of Folarin Balogun’s suspension, said the president of FIFA, Gianni Infantino.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: FIFA’s president, Gianni Infantino, speaking to President Trump during an Oval Office visit in November.

item.title: Spate of Shark Attacks Means More Drones for Australia’s Beaches
item.description: The country is stepping up measures to keep swimmers and surfers safe after a teacher was mauled by a great white shark at Coogee Beach.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times

item.title: NATO Needs Turkey
item.description: The war in Ukraine and Trump’s disregard for the alliance have generated a new appreciation of what Turkey brings to the table.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:description: NATO opens a two-day summit in Ankara, Turkey, today.

item.title: Canada Bets on NATO Partners for Its Expanded Submarine Fleet
item.description: In selecting a joint bid from Germany and Norway, Prime Minister Mark Carney is elevating Canada’s naval power and reducing its military and economic dependency on the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Naval officers from Canada touring a South Korean KSS-III submarine in Esquimalt, British Columbia, in May.

item.title: Iranians Mourn a Slain Leader, and Call for Vengeance
item.description: Million of people filled the streets of Iran to bid farewell to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in the war with the United States and Israel.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Aaron Boxerman, Farnaz Fassihi and Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Crowds gathering around Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s casket in Tehran on Monday. The supreme leader’s body will be taken to several cities before being buried in Mashhad, Iran.

item.title: Heat Waves Are Taking a Big Toll on Chickens
item.description: The birds are particularly vulnerable to high temperatures, and France’s torrid June took a heavy toll.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan and Léontine Gallois
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: At Khamenei’s funeral, mourners call for revenge.
item.description: Mourners
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:description: Signs calling for revenge against President Trump were held up by people attending the funeral procession on Monday.

item.title: Families of Iranians Killed by Government Dismayed by Leader’s Funeral Pageantry
item.description: Critics of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s brutal rule have been angered by the scale of the commemorations being held to honor his life.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi and Rozhin Rezai
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mourners attending the funeral procession of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Monday surround a monument in Tehran.

item.title: Iran to Bury Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Holy City of Mashhad
item.description: The city of three million people is home to a gold-domed shrine that Shiite Muslims revere as Iran’s most sacred site. The final ceremony will take place there on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mashhad is Iran’s second most populous city and where Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was born.

item.title: Wildfires Force Evacuation of Thousands in Southwest France
item.description: The blaze in the Pyrenees, a region bordering Spain, has scorched more than 11,000 acres so far.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: A firefighting jet battling the flames in Ille-sur-Têt, France, on Monday.

item.title: Khamenei’s Burial Was Delayed, Breaking With Islamic Custom
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s remains were preserved for months, according to Iran’s government. Some Islamic scholars say it was a political and security calculation.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iran held a memorial for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in May, but his remains have not been interred despite the Islamic custom of burying people soon after death.

item.title: Sites That Report Venezuela’s Missing Rise Far From Quake Zone
item.description: Despite problems like duplicate submissions, tallies gathered by the websites have been cited by news organizations and critics of the Venezuelan government’s disaster response.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers, relatives and rescue workers search for earthquake survivors under a collapsed public housing complex in Caraballeda, Venezuela, last month, The disaster has bared accuracy problems in how websites gather information on the missing.

item.title: The Iranian authorities go all out to stage an elaborate weeklong spectacle.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners at the Grand Mosalla mosque in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: Khamenei’s funeral draws mourners from across Iran’s regional network.
item.description: From Lebanon to cities across Pakistan, gatherings and marches are expected to show his reach.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners at the Grand Mosalla mosque in Tehran on Saturday for the funeral of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran’s former supreme leader.

item.title: As Mourners for Khamenei Descend on Tehran, Many Residents Take Flight
item.description: Some residents have escaped the city and posted photos of their getaways on social media — a reminder that not all Iranians are mourning the death of Ayatollah Khamenei.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi

item.title: Ukraine Remade Air Defense, but Russia Has Changed Its Attacks
item.description: Ukraine has changed how militaries use Patriot systems. But recent attacks prove that clever adaptations cannot overcome a shortage of interceptors.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady and Liubov Sholudko
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jens Büttner/Picture Alliance, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ukrainian and German soldiers standing near a Patriot air defense system at a training ground in Germany in 2024.

item.title: NATO Leaders Are Meeting This Week. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: As member states gather in Turkey, here’s a guide to the summit, and the decades-old alliance.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: A sign in Ankara, Turkey’s capital, advertising the NATO summit.

item.title: As Trump Cools on NATO, Turkey Hosts Summit Meeting
item.description: As President Trump cools on the alliance, its members see Turkey’s large military and vibrant defense sector as assets.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.media:description: Workers in the Turkish capital, Ankara. Turkey will use the NATO summit to showcase the country’s status as a rising power.

item.title: Zyn’s Popularity Has Tobacco Companies Racing to Cash In
item.description: The makers of nicotine pouches are building new plants and expanding to meet demand. While influencers claim health benefits, experts warn the products can be highly addictive.
item.dc:creator: Murray Carpenter
item.media:credit: Lanna Apisukh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicotine pouch sales are projected to increase to more than $40 billion worldwide by 2033 from $6.9 billion in 2025, one analysis said.

item.title: The World Cup Gave Mexicans a Fantasy They Won’t Soon Forget
item.description: The Mexican team’s early success united and electrified the nation, giving fans a reason to fill the streets in celebration instead of protest.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mexican fans during the World Cup round of 16 match against England at the Azteca Stadium on Sunday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Abdi Latif Dahir

item.title: Andy Burnham, the UK’s Likely Next Prime Minister, Made His Name in Manchester. Here’s How.
item.description: Andy Burnham was mayor of Greater Manchester for nine years. His time there came to define his identity — and his pitch to become prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Andrew Testa
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times

item.title: How Manchester’s Bee Buses and Trams Helped Fuel Andy Burnham’s Rise
item.description: Andy Burnham brought Greater Manchester’s public transit back under public control, making buses more frequent and capping fares.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Bee Network bus at a station in Manchester in June. The worker bee is a longstanding emblem of the city.

item.title: Typhoon Bavi Pounds Guam and Northern Mariana Islands
item.description: There was an islandwide power outage on Saipan, the capital of the Northern Mariana Islands, as the Category 5 storm approached.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar, Josie Moyer and Mar-Vic Cagurangan
item.media:description: Strong winds brought by Super Typhoon Bavi hit Guam on Monday.

item.title: First American Woman Rows Solo From California to Hawaii
item.description: Kelsey Pfendler, a Grand Canyon river-rafting guide, completed the 2,300-mle journey in just under 44 days, according to data from the Ocean Rowing Society International.
item.dc:creator: Evan Gorelick

item.title: Israel’s Government Threatens to Ignore a Top Court Ruling
item.description: A resolution by the country’s cabinet escalated a long-running conflict between Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and the country’s judiciary. A senior government official later walked that back.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Johnatan Reiss
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel at a public event on Sunday with his wife, Sara.

item.title: Can Iran Keep Its Edge?
item.description: Closing the Strait of Hormuz was a huge source of leverage during the war. But playing the same card over and over is risky.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Tehran, last week.

item.title: The Main Attraction at Africa’s Most Lucrative Horse Race? Black Wealth.
item.description: In South Africa, where the Black majority was excluded from the economy for generations, the annual Durban July event is a chance to celebrate luxury and high society.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon and Joao Silva
item.media:description: This is the 130th year of Durban July, a daylong horse racing event in Durban, South Africa. Doris Mataboge, in blue, cheered as her horse won.

item.title: At Site Where Iran’s Slain Leader Lies, Some See a Symbol of His Failures
item.description: The complex where mourners are paying their respects to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei this weekend is unfinished after nearly 40 years. Critics say it is emblematic of the unfulfilled promises of his rule.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners at the funeral for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the Grand Mosalla complex in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: Exhausted by Iran War, Tehran Transforms for Khamenei’s Funeral
item.description: Throughout the capital, the government is using Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s funeral to project strength at a time of great uncertainty for Iran.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Emile Ducke
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: People in central Tehran on Saturday, below an image of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei as a younger man. Portraits of the slain leader can be seen across the capital.

item.title: Mourners Chant ‘Revenge’ at Funeral Prayers for Iran’s Slain Supreme Leader
item.description: On the second day of public mourning for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in February, there was still no sign of his son and successor, Mojtaba Khamenei.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners at the Grand Mosalla in Tehran, on Sunday.

item.title: Far From Kyiv and Moscow, Soldiers Stalk Ruins and Evade Drones on the Front
item.description: Ukraine’s military has denied a Kremlin claim to have taken a city in the eastern Donetsk region, saying its troops are holding out against infiltrating Russians.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Stanislav Kozliuk
item.media:description: A Ukrainian serviceman between the frontline towns of Druzhkivka and Kostiantynivka, in Ukraine’s eastern Donetsk region, last month.

item.title: What We Saw at Ayatollah Khamenei’s Funeral
item.description: Our Middle East correspondent Abdi Latif Dahir attended the first day of funeral ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the former supreme leader of Iran who was killed by U.S. and Israeli air strikes in February.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Michael Anthony Adams, Mikhail Galustov, Coleman Lowndes and Rebecca Suner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Years of Warnings About Public Housing Preceded Venezuela’s Earthquakes
item.description: Residents, construction experts and seismologists said for years that Venezuela’s public housing would be vulnerable in a natural disaster.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz, María Victoria Fermín and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: China Frees Pastor Detained in Crackdown on Religion After Trump’s Urging
item.description: Ezra Jin Mingri was released nearly two months after President Trump raised his case with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, during a visit to Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Pastor Ezra Jin Mingri at the Zion Church in Beijing in 2018. He was detained as part of a wider campaign against religious groups in China.

item.title: China Raises Pressure on Taiwan With Expanded Coast Guard Patrols
item.description: Beijing sent two ships to patrol the waters east of Taiwan, a new campaign to challenge the island’s control of waters crucial to any future invasion.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:description: A Chinese Coast Guard vessel is seen along the coast of Xiamen, a city in China’s southeast. Up to now, Chinese coast guard patrols around Taiwan have mostly been in the strait between the island and the mainland.

item.title: Guam and Northern Mariana Islands Prepare for Typhoon Bavi
item.description: Typhoon Bavi, a tropical cyclone barreling through the Pacific Ocean, was forecast to pass near the U.S. territories on Monday with the force of a Category 5 hurricane.
item.dc:creator: Josie Moyer, Mar-Vic Cagurangan and Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: Mar-Vic Cagurangan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mar-Vic Cagurangan for The New York Times

item.title: Rebel Catholics Defy Vatican’s Calls to Return to Mainstream Church
item.description: The Vatican has excommunicated the priesthood of a rebel Catholic faction. Many of the priests’ followers have refused to renounce them.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: A procession on Wednesday by the traditionalist Society of St. Pius X in Écône, Switzerland.

item.title: Impeachment Trial of Philippine Vice President Set to Begin
item.description: Sara Duterte, who has said she would run for president, could be barred from politics over allegations of corruption and for making death threats against President Ferdinand Marcos Jr.
item.dc:creator: Jason Gutierrez
item.media:description: Vice President Sara Duterte of the Philippines in Manila, in February. Ms. Duterte has been impeached twice, and her trial is set to begin on Monday.

item.title: As Xi Looks to Extend His Rule, He Leans on a Longtime Ally
item.description: Xi Jinping is preparing to extend his rule as China’s leader and bring fresh blood into the Communist Party elite. Cai Qi is the man he’s tasking to help.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.category: Politburo Standing Committee of the Communist Party of China
item.media:description: Cai Qi, a trusted deputy of the Chinese leader, Xi Jinping. He is probably the most influential official in Mr. Xi’s inner circle.

item.title: Denmark’s century-old Fourth of July party looks different this year.
item.description: Overshadowed by President Trump’s threats to seize Greenland, a Danish territory, the event removed U.S. officials from the program and saw protests arrive.
item.dc:creator: Maya Tekeli
item.media:description: Families with picnic lunches beneath Danish and American flags in Rebild Bakker, Denmark, on Saturday.
